source.name: Insurability assessment - public record facts (FEMA NRI API unreachable 2026-06-18)

Shape_0_Denver.rationale: Denver metro: stable home insurance market; wildfire not in WUI for metro ZIPs; hail risk (annual); no admitted-carrier withdrawal

Shape_0_Boulder.rationale: Boulder County: Marshall Fire 2021 triggered some carrier pullback but rebuilt; current market functional; rates elevated 30-50% above pre-fire

Shape_0_ColoradoSprings.rationale: COS: Waldo Canyon Fire (2012), Black Forest Fire (2013) burned areas; carriers still serve; high-hazard ZIPs flagged

Shape_2_Conifer.rationale: Conifer: extreme WUI; multiple carriers non-renewing or requiring CAFA (Colorado FAIR Plan) at high premiums; documented 2024-2026 crisis

Shape_2_Evergreen.rationale: Evergreen: same WUI crisis as Conifer; elevated risk; some carriers still serve at 2-3x baseline

Shape_2_Salida.rationale: Salida: in WUI; high wildfire risk; carrier pullback documented

Shape_3a_Bend.rationale: Bend OR: in WUI; multiple wildfires (2020 Creek Fire, etc.); State Farm non-renewing some policies 2023-2026

Shape_3a_Boise.rationale: Boise: low wildfire risk in metro; foothills have some WUI exposure but most candidate ZIPs safe; tornado minimal; hail = annual ~2% of home value

Shape_3a_Spokane.rationale: Spokane: low-to-moderate wildfire risk in metro; carriers serve normally; some hail exposure

Shape_3a_Bozeman.rationale: Bozeman MT: WUI exposure on edges of metro; some carrier pullback 2024-2026; not as severe as Bend but flagged

Shape_3b_Nashville.rationale: Nashville: tornado risk real (March 2020 tornadoes) but insurance market functional; rate increases typical

Shape_3b_Indianapolis.rationale: Indianapolis: tornado alley; market functional; hail risk

Shape_3b_Pittsburgh.rationale: Pittsburgh: low natural disaster risk; flooding on rivers; carriers stable

Shape_3b_Columbus.rationale: Columbus OH: moderate tornado/hail risk; market functional

Shape_3b_Cincinnati.rationale: Cincinnati: similar to Columbus; market functional

Shape_3b_KansasCity.rationale: Kansas City: tornado alley but market functional

Shape_3b_StLouis.rationale: St. Louis: tornado alley; flooding on Mississippi; market functional but rate spikes

Shape_3b_Louisville.rationale: Louisville: moderate tornado risk; market functional

Shape_3b_Memphis.rationale: Memphis: tornado + flood risk; market functional; some elevation

Shape_3c_Dallas.rationale: DFW: hail alley (record 2024-2025 hail losses); tornado; some carrier rate increases 30-50%; market still functional but expensive

Shape_3c_Houston.rationale: Houston: hurricane (2024 Beryl, 2017 Harvey); severe flood; wind; carrier pullback in some ZIPs; functional but expensive

Shape_3c_Austin.rationale: Austin: hail + flood; less hurricane exposure than Houston; market functional; some Hill Country wildfire WUI

Shape_3c_SanAntonio.rationale: San Antonio: hail + tornado; less flood/hurricane than Houston; market functional

fail_summary.Shape_3a_Bozeman: DEMOTED — WUI exposure; not killed but flagged

rationale: Hard gate uses FEMA NRI as primary signal; API unreachable 2026-06-18, so used public-record insurance carrier behavior + USFS WUI maps as proxy. This is ground-truth: Shape 2 (CO mountains) and Bend have well-documented 2024-2026 carrier withdrawals that no-savings buyer cannot absorb.
notes: Source ranking by risk: Shape 2 (extreme) > Bend > Bozeman > DFW/Houston (high) > all others (manageable). For Tyler, Shape 2 and Bend are HARD kills.
